questions.question: Which skill is best suited for a full-blown panic attack with a racing heart, dizziness, and feeling completely out of control?

options.text: 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding, to redirect attention to your senses
Riding the Wave, to observe the feeling with curiosity
TIPP, to rapidly change your body chemistry out of the red zone
Deep thinking about the cause of the panic

questions.explanation: TIPP is designed for physiological emergencies — panic attacks, overwhelming rage, or moments where you feel completely out of control. It works by rapidly changing your body chemistry to bring you out of the red zone, which is what a full-blown crisis requires.

questions.question: You are lying awake in bed with your mind replaying a stressful conversation from earlier. Which skill is the best match?

options.text: TIPP — do intense exercise to burn off the anxious energy
5-4-3-2-1 Grounding — redirect your attention from the thought spiral to your senses
Riding the Wave — observe the stress with curiosity
No skill is helpful for sleep-time anxiety

questions.explanation: A mind stuck in a loop replaying past events is a thought spiral — and that is exactly what 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding is built for. In bed, you can notice the weight of your blanket, sounds in the room, or the faint smell of your pillow to anchor yourself in the present moment.

questions.question: You just received disappointing news and feel a deep wave of sadness — strong, but not a crisis. Which skill is the best fit?

options.text: TIPP — dip your face in cold water to slow your heart rate
5-4-3-2-1 Grounding — list things you can see and touch
Riding the Wave — name the feeling, observe it in your body, and let it pass naturally
Intense exercise — burn off the emotional energy immediately

questions.explanation: Riding the Wave is designed for strong but manageable emotions like sadness, frustration, or disappointment. By naming the feeling and observing it with curiosity instead of fighting it, you allow the wave to pass naturally rather than making it bigger.

questions.question: According to the lesson's decision framework, what should you check first when deciding which skill to use?

options.text: Your thoughts — are they racing or stuck in a loop?
Your emotions — how strong are they on a scale of 1 to 10?
Your body — are you in a physiological crisis with a racing heart or shaking?
Your environment — are you somewhere private or in public?

questions.explanation: The framework starts with your body. If you are in a physiological crisis — heart racing, shaking, completely overwhelmed — start with TIPP. Only after checking your body do you move on to checking your mind for thought spirals or your emotions for manageable waves.

questions.question: Describe a real situation from your past week where you could have used one of the three skills. Which skill would you choose — TIPP, 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding, or Riding the Wave — and what would the very first step look like in that specific moment?
questions.explanation: Connecting these skills to your real life is what builds the habit of reaching for them. The more specific you are — naming the exact situation and the exact first action — the more likely you are to actually use the skill when a similar moment arrives.

title: DBT Skills Challenge: Choosing the Right Tool - Quiz
